metadata.description: Essential Chinese vocabulary for greetings, introductions, and basic conversation starters

content.name: you
content.side_g: Informal singular 'you'; the polite/respectful form is 您 (nín), used for elders, teachers, and strangers. 你好 is the universal greeting at any time of day.

content.name: good
content.side_a: fine; good; nice; O.K.; it's settled
content.side_g: Standard response to 你好吗? ('How are you?'). Mandarin adjectives usually require a degree adverb — 很 (hěn, 'very') is grammatically obligatory here even without intensifying meaning. Alone, 好! functions as 'OK!' or 'Alright!'

content.name: please
content.side_a: please (polite form of request); to treat or to invite (somebody)
content.side_g: Placed before a verb to make a polite request ('please do X'). Also a standalone verb meaning to invite or treat someone (e.g., 我请你吃饭 — 'I'll treat you to a meal'). 请问 is the most common fixed phrase.

content.name: ask
content.side_f: Excuse me, what is your (honorable) surname?
content.side_g: 问 covers asking questions; distinct from 要 (requesting things) and 叫 (calling/naming). 请问 is a fixed polite opener when approaching a stranger — always placed at the start of the question.

content.name: honorable
content.side_g: Literally 'expensive,' but used as an honorific prefix when inquiring about someone's name. 贵姓 is the formal 'what's your surname?' — the reply drops 贵 entirely: 我姓王 ('My surname is Wang').

content.name: surname
content.side_a: (one's) surname is...; to be surnamed; surname
content.side_g: 姓 works as both noun ('surname') and verb ('to be surnamed'); the verbal use is standard, e.g. 我姓王 ('my surname is Wang'), never *我的姓是王 in casual speech. The set phrase 贵姓 is the polite way to ask someone's family name, answered with 我姓X (not 我贵姓). Distinguish 姓 (family name only) from 名字 (given name or full name).

content.name: i_me
content.side_f: My surname is Wang. (lit. 'I surname Wang')
content.side_g: 我 wǒ means 'I/me' and works as both subject and object — Chinese pronouns don't change form by case. It's used with the 我姓X pattern to state your surname. Add 们 to form the plural 我们 (wǒmen, 'we/us').

content.name: question_particle
content.side_e: Nǐ hǎo! Wǒ hěn hǎo. Nǐ ne?
content.side_f: Hello! I'm very well. And you? (lit. 'you NE?')
content.side_g: 呢 (ne) is a sentence-final particle that bounces a question back to the listener or asks about a contextual situation, as in 'Nǐ ne?' = 'And you?'. Unlike 吗 (ma), which turns a statement into a yes/no question, 呢 relies on prior context and cannot be answered with just yes or no. It always sits at the very end of the sentence, right after the noun or topic it questions.

content.name: miss
content.side_e: Qǐng wèn, xiǎojiě, nín guì xìng?
content.side_f: Excuse me, Miss, what is your surname?
content.side_g: 小姐 is used as a polite title after a surname (e.g., 王小姐 'Miss Wang') and works well in service, business, and formal address, especially in Taiwan, Hong Kong, and among older speakers. In mainland China it has acquired slang connotations (sometimes implying a sex worker), so addressing a stranger as just '小姐' can feel awkward or rude; 女士 (nǚshì, 'Madam/Ms.') is a safer neutral alternative. Using it with a surname (王小姐) remains broadly acceptable everywhere.

content.name: call
content.side_e: Wǒ jiào Lǐ Yǒu, nǐ ne?
content.side_f: I'm called Li You, and you? (lit. 'I called Li You, you [question-particle]?')
content.side_g: Use 我叫… to introduce your given name or full name (我叫李友 = 'I'm called Li You'), whereas 我姓… introduces only your surname (我姓李 = 'My surname is Li'). 叫 can also mean 'to call (out to)' or 'to summon,' as in calling someone over. It is less formal than 姓 and is the standard way Chinese speakers give their name in casual introductions.

content.name: what
content.side_f: What are you called? (lit. 'You called what?')
content.side_g: 什么 (shénme) occupies the same slot as the answer — no inversion like in English (你叫什么? not 什么你叫?). Frequent collocations: 什么名字 (what name), 什么时候 (when, lit. 'what time'), 什么意思 (what meaning). The 么 is always neutral tone (shén·me).

content.name: name
content.side_f: What is your name? (lit. 'You called what name?')
content.side_g: 名字 (míngzi) means 'name,' typically referring to one's given name or full name, and is distinct from 姓 (xìng), which refers specifically to the surname/family name. The second syllable -zi carries a neutral tone, a common suffix pattern in Mandarin nouns. The canonical introduction 你叫什么名字? pairs 名字 with 叫 and 什么 to ask someone's name in a friendly, everyday register.

content.name: mister
content.side_f: Mr. Wang, what is your honorable surname?
content.side_g: Used after a surname as a polite title (王先生 = Mr. Wang) and can also mean 'husband' in context. The second syllable -sheng is pronounced in the neutral tone. It is a respectful way to address adult men, and in classical usage it also meant 'teacher.'

content.name: li_you
content.side_g: In Chinese name order, the surname comes first: 李 (Lǐ) is the family name and 友 (Yǒu) is the given name. Li You is the female student protagonist of Integrated Chinese Lesson 1, who meets and befriends 王朋 (Wáng Péng). Introduce yourself by saying 我叫… followed by your name.

content.name: wang_peng
content.side_e: Wǒ xìng Wáng, jiào Wáng Péng.
content.side_f: My surname is Wang; I'm called Wang Peng.
content.side_g: 王 (Wáng) is one of the most common Chinese surnames, historically meaning 'king.' Chinese names place the surname first (王) followed by the given name (朋), so 王朋 is addressed as Wang Peng, not Peng Wang. He is the male student protagonist of the Integrated Chinese textbook series who meets 李友 (Li You) in Lesson 1.
